1: The new oracular formula introduces Ezekiel 34, which contains one of the most influential prophetic oracles in the OT: the shepherd discourse. The metaphor of ruler-as-shepherd was ubiquitous in ancient Near Eastern royal ideology. Hammurabi (18th c. BCE) called himself “the shepherd of the black-headed people.” Egyptian pharaohs carried the crook (heqa) as the shepherd’s staff symbol of royal authority. The Sumerian concept of the king as shepherd (sipa) governed Mesopotamian royal self-presentation from at least the 3rd millennium BCE. Ezekiel mobilizes this common ANE metaphor to indict Israel’s ruling class (kings, nobles, priests) for failure to perform their shepherding obligations.
2: “Woe to the shepherds of Israel who feed themselves!” — the indictment begins with the rhetorical hoi (woe) formula, typically used for funeral laments or prophetic condemnations (Isaiah 5:8-23; Ezekiel 13:3, 18). The shepherds feed (ro-im) themselves; the flock they do not feed. The Hebrew root ra-ah (to shepherd, to graze, to tend) is used for both the shepherds’ self-feeding and the failing of the flock — the same activity directed at themselves rather than at those entrusted to them. The oracle is addressed to the class of leaders responsible for Jerusalem’s fall: kings (Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, Zedekiah) and the ruling establishment.
3: The catalog of self-enrichment at the flock’s expense: eating the fat (helev, the choicest portion), wearing the wool, slaughtering the fatlings — all the best products of the flock are consumed by the shepherd rather than used to care for the sheep. The fat, wool, and choice animals were the economically valuable outputs of the flock. The metaphor maps precisely onto the Jerusalem ruling class’s extraction of resources from the population: the prophetic literature consistently condemns the wealthy’s exploitation of the poor (Amos 2:6-8; Isaiah 3:14-15; Micah 3:1-3).
4: The fourfold failure of the shepherds stated as a comprehensive catalog of neglect: (1) “You have not strengthened the weak (lo chizzaqtem la-chalashim)” — the weak were left without reinforcement; (2) “healed the sick (lo refattem la-cholah)” — the diseased received no treatment; (3) “bound up the injured (la-nishberet lo chabashtem)” — the broken limb got no splinting; (4) “brought back the strayed (hanidachat lo hashevothem) or sought the lost (ha-avedat lo viqqashtem).” The catalog is a negative checklist of the ideal shepherd’s duties — Jesus’s self-description in John 10 and the Good Samaritan parable in Luke 10 are direct echoes of this passage’s imagery. “With force and harshness you have ruled them (bechazaqah uvepharech reditem otam)” — pharech (harshness, cruelty) is the word used in Exodus 1:13-14 for Egypt’s ruthless treatment of Israel in slavery. The leaders have become Egypt to their own people.
5: “They were scattered because there was no shepherd (ein ro-eh)” — the direct cause of Israel’s dispersion is the absence of true leadership. The sheep “became food for every wild beast” (la-achlah lekhol-chayat hassadeh) — the predatory nations that devoured the leaderless population. The scattering (vayyaputzu) is both the geographic reality of the Babylonian dispersion and the theological consequence of covenant abandonment: YHWH had warned in the Deuteronomic curses that Israel would be scattered among the nations (Deuteronomy 28:64).
6: “My sheep wandered over all the mountains and on every high hill” — note the possessive: these are “my sheep” (tzoni, YHWH’s flock). The contrast with the “shepherds of Israel” is deliberate: the human shepherds (Israel’s kings) have failed YHWH’s flock, not merely a human flock. The mountains and high hills are the geographic features of Canaan’s landscape, evoking the widespread dispersion across the land before the final deportation. “With no one to search or seek for them (lo dorush velo mevaqqesh)” — the two verbs (darash, to inquire/seek diligently; baqash, to seek) mirror the verbs YHWH will use of himself in Ezekiel 34:11, creating the rhetorical contrast between the absent human shepherds and the seeking divine shepherd.
7: The legal summons formula: “Therefore, you shepherds, hear the word of the LORD.” The word lachen (therefore) introduces the consequential judgment following the indictment of Ezekiel 34:2-6. The legal proceedings against the shepherds require a formal hearing formula to separate the accusation from the verdict. The formula “hear the word of the LORD (shim-u devar YHWH)” is used throughout the prophets to introduce covenantal pronouncements to specific audiences (Amos 5:1; Jeremiah 2:4).
8: YHWH’s oath of indictment: “As I live (chai ani), because my flock has become a prey and food for all the wild beasts, since there was no shepherd, and because my shepherds have not searched for my flock but have fed themselves rather than feeding my flock.” The protracted subordinate clause — the “because” clause that fills the entire verse — makes the causal connection explicit: the predation of the flock is directly traceable to the shepherds’ self-service and failure to seek. The oath formula guarantees that the following verdict (Ezekiel 34:10) will be executed without qualification.
9: The second summons of the shepherds to hear the word of YHWH repeats Ezekiel 34:7 verbatim, creating the Hebrew rhetorical pattern of double announcement before a significant declaration. The repetition is not redundant but emphatic — the formal juridical structure of a legal proceeding requires that the accused be clearly addressed before the verdict is read. The shepherds are explicitly identified as the defendants in YHWH’s court.
10: “Behold I am against the shepherds (hineni el-haro-im)” — the adversative formula hineni el (I am against) is Ezekiel’s characteristic announcement of divine opposition to a specific entity (Ezekiel 13:8; Ezekiel 21:8; Ezekiel 26:3; Ezekiel 28:22). “I will hold them accountable for my flock (uviqashti et-tzoni mi-yadam)” — the verb baqash (to seek, to require) used earlier for the shepherds’ failure to seek the scattered flock is now turned against the shepherds: YHWH will require (baqash) the flock from their hand. The juridical reversal is precise. “I will rescue my flock from their mouths that it may not be food for them” — the same “food” vocabulary (Ezekiel 34:5) is now applied to the shepherds themselves eating the sheep, not the wild beasts.
11: “For thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I myself will search for my sheep and seek them out (vedarashti et-tzoni uveqartim).” The emphatic “I myself (ani)” marks the climactic shift from the failed human shepherds to the divine shepherd who personally takes up the task. The two verbs (darash, to search diligently; baqar, to seek/examine) are the mirror of the human shepherds’ failure in Ezekiel 34:6 (“no one to search or seek”). YHWH’s personal assumption of the shepherding role is the radical claim of the passage: the divine king of Israel will do what Israel’s human kings failed to do. John 10:11 (“I am the good shepherd”) is the NT’s claim that Jesus is the enactment of this YHWH-as-shepherd promise in human form.
12: “As a shepherd seeks out his flock when some of his sheep have been scattered, so will I seek out my sheep.” The simile draws on pastoral practice: the owner of a scattered flock personally combs the terrain to locate each missing animal. “I will rescue them from all the places where they have been scattered on a day of clouds and thick darkness (beyom anan va-arafel).” The phrase “day of clouds and thick darkness” is the vocabulary of theophany (Exodus 19:9; Deuteronomy 4:11) and divine judgment (Joel 2:2; Zephaniah 1:15) — the gathering will happen precisely in the moment of historical catastrophe, not despite it but through it.
13: “I will bring them out from the peoples and gather them from the countries (vegibbatstim min-ha-aratzot)” — the new exodus/restoration language that governs Ezekiel 33-48. The gathering of the dispersed (qibbuts galuyot, ingathering of exiles) is the prophetic program announced here and developed in Ezekiel 36-37. “I will bring them into their own land. I will feed them on the mountains of Israel, by the streams, and in all the inhabited places.” The mountains of Israel — which in Ezekiel 35 will be contrasted with Mount Seir’s desolation — become the site of restoration pastoral abundance.
14: “I will feed them in good pasture, and their fold shall be on the mountain heights of Israel (bemaromi Yisrael).” The mountain heights of Israel are the geographic inverse of the “every high hill” on which the sheep wandered leaderless in Ezekiel 34:6. The restoration involves returning to the same terrain, but under YHWH’s direct shepherding rather than human negligence. “On rich pasture (mireh shamen) they shall feed on the mountains of Israel.” The restored covenant community will enjoy the actual material blessing of the promised land — the covenant promise of Deuteronomy 8:7-9 fulfilled after the judgment period.
15: “I myself will be the shepherd of my flock (ani er-eh tzoni), and I myself will make them lie down (ani arbi-itsem), declares the Lord GOD.” The doubled ani (I myself) is emphatic repetition for rhetorical force: YHWH is not delegating the shepherding role but personally performing it. The phrase “make them lie down” (arbi-itsem) evokes Psalms 23:2 (“He makes me lie down in green pastures”) — Psalm 23 is the lyrical meditation on exactly this promise, where YHWH-as-shepherd performs each action that Israel’s failed shepherds neglected. The oracle of Ezekiel 34 is the juridical-prophetic prose behind the Psalm’s poetic confessions.
16: YHWH’s shepherd duties as the positive fulfillment of the negative catalog of the human shepherds’ failures (Ezekiel 34:4): “I will seek the lost, bring back the strayed, bind up the injured, and strengthen the weak.” Each item directly reverses the failure-catalog. “But the fat and the strong I will destroy (ve-hashemen ve-hachazaq eshamed); I will feed them in justice (mishpat).” The intra-flock judgment — between those who exploited the weak and the vulnerable who were exploited — introduces the second movement of the chapter (Ezekiel 34:17-22). YHWH’s shepherding is not mere benevolence but includes active justice on behalf of the marginalized.
17: “As for you, my flock, thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I judge between sheep and sheep, between rams and male goats.” The oracle extends beyond the external human shepherds to the internal dynamics of the community itself. The “rams and male goats” (eilim u-attudim) — the dominant animals within the flock — represent the powerful members of the covenant community who took advantage of their position. In pastoral practice, dominant rams push less dominant sheep away from water and pasture. The social-class application is clear: the wealthy and powerful within Israel exploited the weak and poor.
18: “Is it not enough for you to feed on the good pasture, that you must tread down with your feet the rest of your pasture?” The metaphor of fouling the water supply and trampling the pasture captures the social reality of elite economic behavior: not merely consuming the good, but actively destroying what others could have used. Muddy water and trampled pasture are useless to those who come after the dominant animals. The economic application: the wealthy not only monopolized resources but destroyed the productive capacity of the commons through their practices.
19: “Must my flock eat what you have trodden with your feet, and drink what you have muddied with your feet?” The rhetorical question completes the social-class analysis: the poor receive the remnants degraded by the rich — trampled food and fouled water. This imagery is the pastoral-allegorical expression of the prophetic social critique that runs from Amos through Isaiah through Jeremiah: the economic arrangements of pre-exile Israel systematically disadvantaged the poor while benefiting the rich. Ezekiel places the responsibility for this directly on the dominant members of the community.
20: “Therefore, thus says the Lord GOD to them: Behold, I myself will judge between the fat sheep and the lean sheep.” The lachen (therefore) introduces YHWH’s personal judicial intervention in the intra-community social conflict. “The fat sheep and the lean sheep” (bein seh briah lish-seh razah) — fat (well-fed, economically privileged) versus lean (malnourished, economically marginalized). The divine judge takes the side of the lean sheep, not because they are morally superior, but because YHWH’s covenant justice includes active vindication of the marginalized against those who exploited them.
21: “Because you push with side and shoulder, and thrust at all the weak with your horns (qarneikem tenachu)” — the physical behavior of dominant rams in a flock, used to describe the social aggression of the powerful members of the community. The horn (qeren) is the symbol of power throughout the OT (Psalms 75:4-5; Daniel 7:8; Amos 6:13). “Until you have scattered them abroad (pitzztam el-chutz)” — the rich have contributed to the scattering of the poor through economic predation, mirroring (and perhaps causing) the national scattering that YHWH judged.
22: “I will rescue my flock, and they shall no longer be a prey (levo-az liviz).” The promise of protection extends the shepherd imagery to its covenantal fullness: the restored community will not be subject to either external predators (other nations) or internal exploiters (the powerful within the community). “I will judge between sheep and sheep” — the repeated formula (first in Ezekiel 34:17, now in Ezekiel 34:22) closes the intra-community judgment section and introduces the Messianic promise of Ezekiel 34:23-24. The establishment of justice within the community is the precondition for the appointment of the shepherd-king.
23: “And I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David (eved david), and he shall feed them.” After the long account of YHWH’s personal shepherding, this verse introduces the human agent through whom that shepherding will be exercised: “my servant David.” David had been dead for four centuries by Ezekiel’s time; “my servant David” is therefore the Messianic figure who inherits David’s royal office and covenant promises (2 Samuel 7:12-16: “I will raise up your offspring after you”). The Davidic covenant is the framework: the one shepherd is not David personally but the Davidic heir who fulfils what David’s dynasty was meant to be. Isaiah 11:1 (“a shoot from the stump of Jesse”), Jeremiah 23:5 (“a righteous Branch for David”), and Zechariah 3:8; Ezekiel 6:12 (“my servant the Branch”) all operate within this same Davidic-Messianic promise framework.
24: “And I, the LORD, will be their God, and my servant David shall be nasi (prince, leader) among them.” The term nasi (prince, leader, elevated one) is Ezekiel’s preferred title for the Messianic ruler rather than melech (king) — Ezekiel uses nasi for the restored Davidic leader throughout the temple vision (Ezekiel 44:3; Ezekiel 45:7-17; Ezekiel 46:2-18; Ezekiel 48:21-22). The distinction may reflect a deliberate distancing from the failed melech-ship of Judah’s kings: the restored leader will be a nasi under YHWH’s kingship rather than an autonomous melech. The covenant formula “I will be their God” (ani YHWH eloheichem) closes the verse with the relational core of all the promises.
25: “I will make a covenant of peace (berit shalom) with them.” The covenant of peace appears in Ezekiel at 34:25 and 37:26 and in Isaiah at 54:10 and Ezekiel 66:12 — it is the eschatological covenant that fulfills and exceeds the Mosaic covenant. The berit shalom (covenant of peace/completeness/well-being) uses shalom in its full sense of total well-being, not merely the absence of conflict. “Banish wild beasts from the land (vehishhbati chayah ra-ah min-ha-aretz)” — wild animals as a covenant-curse symbol (Leviticus 26:22: “I will send wild beasts against you”; Ezekiel 14:15) are now removed as a covenant-blessing sign. The wilderness becomes safe, the forest navigable.
26: “I will make them and the places around my hill a blessing (berachah), and I will send down the showers in their season (geshem bito).” The seasonal rains are the most fundamental blessing in Palestine’s agricultural calendar: the early rains (October-November) for planting and the latter rains (March-April) for grain. Their regularity was the covenant blessing of Deuteronomy 11:14 (“I will give the rain of your land in its season”) and their withholding the curse (Deuteronomy 28:24; 1 Kings 17’s drought under Ahab). “Showers of blessing (gishme berachah yihyu)” — the phrase is deliberately comprehensive: the rain itself is theologically characterized as blessing, not merely as meteorological fact.
27: “The trees of the field shall yield their fruit, and the earth shall yield its increase.” The restored land’s agricultural productivity is the material expression of covenant restoration (Leviticus 26:4: “the land shall yield its increase and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit”). “They shall be secure in their land. And they shall know that I am the LORD, when I break the bars of their yoke (beshivri et-motot ullam).” The “bars of the yoke” is the standard ANE metaphor for political subjugation under a foreign power (Jeremiah 27:2-12: the yoke of Babylon). Breaking the yoke = release from political servitude = recognition of YHWH as the one who acts in history to liberate his people.
28: “They shall no longer be a prey to the nations, nor shall the beasts of the land devour them.” The double security from both political (nations) and natural (wild beasts) threats completes the reversal of the covenant curses (Leviticus 26:22, 25). “They shall dwell securely (lavetech) and none shall make them afraid (ein machared).” The phrase lavetech... ein machared is the formula of covenant security that appears in Leviticus 26:6 and throughout the prophetic restoration oracles (Jeremiah 23:6; Ezekiel 33:16; Micah 4:4; Zephaniah 3:13). Security from fear is the fundamental experiential quality of restored covenant life.
29: “And I will provide for them a renowned planting (matta leshemah), so that they shall no more be consumed with famine in the land.” Matta leshemah (a planting for/of reputation, a renowned plantation) — the phrase is unique to Ezekiel and difficult. It likely refers to an established, productive agricultural community whose abundance is recognized by surrounding nations (“renowned” in the sense of known for its fruitfulness). “No longer suffer the disgrace of the nations (lo yis-u od cherpat hagoyim)” — the shame of exile and dispossession has been the national disgrace; the restoration removes it. Honor-shame was not merely an emotional category in ancient Israel but a social reality with economic and political dimensions.
30: “They shall know that I, the LORD their God, am with them (otam), and that they, the house of Israel, are my people (ami), declares the Lord GOD.” The mutual covenant formula (“I am their God / they are my people”) is the relational core of all the preceding material restoration (land, rain, security, food). It is the Sinai covenant formula (Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12) given its eschatological fullness: the mechanical national covenant becomes a known relational reality. The NT fulfillment is the Incarnation and Pentecost: Emmanuel (God-with-us) and the Spirit’s indwelling are the “I am with them” realized in person and power.
31: “You are my sheep, human sheep of my pasture (tzon mar-iti tzon adam atten), and I am your God, declares the Lord GOD.” The closing verse’s phrase tzon adam (human sheep, literally “sheep of humans / humankind”) may be distinguishing Israel from literal sheep while maintaining the metaphor, or emphasizing the personal relational ownership: human beings who are YHWH’s flock, not merely livestock. The closing covenant declaration “I am your God” in the mouth of YHWH directly echoes “I am the LORD your God” from the Decalogue (Exodus 20:2) — the shepherd oracle ends where the covenant began: with YHWH’s self-identifying declaration of covenant ownership.
